md: The **normal distribution** (or **bell curve**) is the most important continuous distribution in statistics. Many real-world quantities — heights, test scores, measurement errors — are approximately normal.
## 1. Anatomy of the Bell Curve
A normal distribution is described by two parameters:
**Mean $\mu$** — the center. The peak of the bell.
**Standard deviation $\sigma$** — the spread. Larger $\sigma$ = wider, flatter curve.
**Notation:** $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. (Some textbooks use $N(\mu, \sigma)$ — check yours.)
Try the widget below. Slide $\mu$ to shift the curve left/right; slide $\sigma$ to make it wider or narrower.
## 2. The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)
For *any* normal distribution:
**About $68\%$** of the data falls within $1\sigma$ of the mean.
**About $95\%$** falls within $2\sigma$.
**About $99.7\%$** falls within $3\sigma$.
**This is universal** — works for any normal distribution regardless of $\mu$ or $\sigma$.

blocks.title: The 68-95-99.7 rule in action
blocks.body: **SAT scores** are designed to be approximately normal with $\mu = 1000$ and $\sigma = 200$. So about 68% score between 800 and 1200. About 95% between 600 and 1400. About 99.7% between 400 and 1600. A score above 1600 (or below 400) is extremely rare.

md: ## 3. The z-Score (Standardization)
A **z-score** measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean.
**$z > 0$:** above the mean. **$z < 0$:** below. **$z = 0$:** at the mean.
**Example.** SAT $\mu = 1000, \sigma = 200$. Your score: $1300$.
$z = (1300 - 1000) / 200 = 1.5$. You scored **1.5 standard deviations above the mean**.
z-scores let you compare data from different normal distributions on the same scale.
## 4. The Standard Normal
When you standardize ($z$-score everything), you get the **standard normal distribution**: $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$. Written $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.
Probabilities for the standard normal are tabulated (z-tables) or built into calculators. For any other normal, convert to z-scores first.
### Worked example — using the empirical rule
Adult male heights are approximately $N(70, 3)$ inches.
**About what fraction are between 67 and 73 inches?** This is $\mu \pm 1\sigma = 70 \pm 3$. By the empirical rule: **about 68%**.
**Above 76 inches?** That's $\mu + 2\sigma$. About 95% are within $\pm 2\sigma$, so 5% are outside. Symmetry → about **2.5%** above 76 inches.
**Below 64 inches?** $\mu - 2\sigma$. Same logic — **about 2.5%**.
### Worked example — z-scores
Test 1: $\mu = 75, \sigma = 10$. You score 88.
Test 2: $\mu = 60, \sigma = 5$. You score 70.
Which is the better performance?
Test 2 is the better performance — you're 2 standard deviations above the mean, vs only 1.3 on Test 1.
### Worked example — using the widget
What's $P(X < 1)$ for the standard normal $N(0, 1)$? Set up the widget with mean=0, sd=1, region=`X < a`, a=1. The shaded area is the probability — about **0.8413**.
What's $P(-1 < X < 1)$? Set region to 'between', a=-1, b=1. Area should be about **0.6827** — that's the 68% in the empirical rule, computed exactly!
## 5. Computing Probabilities with a z-Table
For non-round z-scores (anything other than 0, ±1, ±2, ±3), the empirical rule isn't precise enough. We need actual values of $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ — the standard-normal CDF.
Modern usage: calculators and statistical software have it built in. Historical / handbook usage: a printed *z-table*. Either way, here are the values you'll see most often:

blocks.title: Key values of Φ(z) = P(Z ≤ z)

md: ### Worked example — using the table
**Step 1.** Standardize: $z = (78 - 70)/5 = 1.6$.
**Step 2.** Look up $\Phi(1.6)$. The table gives $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$ and $\Phi(2.0) = 0.9772$. Either interpolate to $\Phi(1.6) \approx 0.9452$, or accept the nearest available value.
**Step 3.** Done: $P(X < 78) \approx 0.945$.
## 6. Reverse: Percentile → z-score
Sometimes you know the probability and want to find the $z$. Read the table BACKWARDS:
**Example.** 90th percentile of the standard normal — find $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.90$.
Scan the table: $\Phi(1.28) \approx 0.9$. So the 90th percentile $z \approx 1.28$.
**Apply to non-standard normal.** For SAT $\sim N(1000, 200)$, the 90th percentile is at $x = \mu + z \sigma = 1000 + 1.28 \cdot 200 \approx 1256$. So $90\%$ of SAT scores are below $\approx 1256$.

blocks.title: Critical values you'll see again
blocks.body:
Certain $z$-values appear all the time in statistics — memorize these:

| Percentile | z-value |
|---|---|
| 90% (one-tail) | 1.28 |
| 95% (one-tail) | 1.645 |
| 95% (two-tail, ±) | 1.96 |
| 99% (one-tail) | 2.326 |
| 99% (two-tail, ±) | 2.576 |

The two-tail $1.96$ is the most famous — it's the basis of $95\%$ confidence intervals.

md: ## 7. Why the Normal Is Everywhere
Heights, test scores, measurement errors, IQs, blood pressures, weights — countless real-world quantities are approximately normal. Why?

**Central Limit Theorem (CLT).** When a quantity is the SUM (or average) of many independent small contributions, its distribution becomes approximately normal — regardless of the shape of the individual contributions.
**Height** = sum of many genetic + environmental factors → approximately normal.
**Test scores** = sum of points across many questions → approximately normal.
**Measurement error** = sum of many tiny random perturbations → approximately normal.
(The Galton board topic visualizes the CLT directly: each marble's bin = sum of independent ± choices, and the histogram converges to a bell curve.)

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Normal distribution = bell curve, parameters $\mu, \sigma$. **Empirical rule:** 68% within $1\sigma$, 95% within $2\sigma$, 99.7% within $3\sigma$. **z-score:** $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ — measures distance from mean in standard deviations. Standardize to compare across different normal distributions. **Key table values:** $\Phi(1) \approx 0.84, \Phi(1.96) = 0.975, \Phi(2) \approx 0.98, \Phi(2.58) \approx 0.995$. **CLT** explains why so much real-world data is approximately normal.
